rows.title: MarginalizationAK
rows.tips: REFACTOR row. This row is a refactor ROOT in `marginalize_loose_self_cycle` (roots: `def_3_14`). rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_index_boundary_excludes_natural_cases] The "bifurcation" in (iv) is to be interpreted according to the previously formalized definition of bifurcation, not by the literal index range $w_1, \dots, w_{n-1}$ displayed in the walk. In particular, with the convention $w_0 := \ul{v}$ and $w_n := \ol{v}$, the bidirected-edge position index $k$ ranges over $\{1, \dots, n\}$, so that $n=1$ (a direct bidirected edge $\ul{v} \huh \ol{v}$ already present in $L$ with no intermediates), $n=2, k=1$ (a "Y" $\ul{v} \hus w_1 \tuh \ol{v}$), and $n=2, k=n$ (the mirror "Y" $\ul{v} \hut w_1 \hus \ol{v}$) all qualify as bifurcations witnessing membership in $L^{\sm W}$, provided the intermediate nodes (if any) lie in $W$.

[self_cycle_asymmetry_between_directed_and_bidirected] The asymmetry between clauses (iii) and (iv) regarding self-edges is intentional and shall be preserved by any formalization: directed self-cycles $v \tuh v$ may appear in $E^{\sm W}$ (as flagged by the footnote), while bidirected self-edges $v \huh v$ are excluded from $L^{\sm W}$ by the explicit $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ constraint in clause (iv). A formalization should neither impose $\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$ on $E^{\sm W}$ nor relax it on $L^{\sm W}$.

Additionally, in clause (iii), a self-cycle $v \tuh v$ is admitted to $E^{\sm W}$ only when witnessed by a directed walk of length at least 2 (i.e., $n \geq 2$, equivalently with at least one intermediate node $w_i \in W$). A length-1 walk (a direct edge $v \tuh v$ already present in $G$, with no intermediates) does not on its own justify a self-cycle in $E^{\sm W}$. For non-self edges ($\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$), length-1 walks remain admissible.

wording_check.output:
Looking at the tex block for def_3_14 (MarginalizationAK), I need to audit the literal wording for subtleties, ambiguities, and corner cases.

Let me analyze each component carefully, focusing on what the LN literally says.

```
SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_indexing_unclear_at_k_boundaries
explanation: The bifurcation formula in item iv

    \[\ul{v} \hut w_1 \hut \cdots \hut w_{k-1} \hus w_k \tuh \cdots \tuh w_{n-1} \tuh \ol{v}, \]

uses indices $w_{k-1}$ and $w_k$ to name the endpoints of the bidirected hinge. The "(if any)" qualifier addresses the case when the intermediate list $w_1,\dots,w_{n-1}$ is empty, but says nothing about the hinge indices when they fall outside that list.

For $k = 1$, the hinge "$w_{k-1} \hus w_k$" literally reads "$w_0 \hus w_1$", and $w_0$ is undefined in the intermediate range. The intended reading is that $w_0$ becomes $\ul{v}$ implicitly — so the bifurcation is $\ul{v} \hus w_1 \tuh \cdots \tuh \ol{v}$. Symmetrically, when $k = n$, $w_n$ implicitly becomes $\ol{v}$, giving $\ul{v} \hut w_1 \hut \cdots \hut w_{n-1} \hus \ol{v}$. And when $n = 1, k = 1$, both arms are empty and the bifurcation degenerates to $\ul{v} \hus \ol{v}$ — a direct bidirected edge in $G$.

This boundary case is not cosmetic: it's the only way a direct bidirected edge $\ul{v} \huh \ol{v}$ in $L$ (where $\ul{v},\ol{v} \in V \sm W$) ever gets captured by a bifurcation and preserved in $L^{\sm W}$. If a strict reader instead requires $w_{k-1}, w_k$ to be in the intermediate range $\{1,\dots,n-1\}$, the boundary cases are excluded and original bidirected edges between non-marginalized nodes are silently dropped — clearly not intended.

The convention "$w_0 = \ul{v}$, $w_n = \ol{v}$" is left implicit, and a strict reading of the formula admits this confusion. Downstream claims about preservation of edges under the trivial marginalization ($W = \emptyset$) or about subset relationships of bifurcations would rely on this implicit convention.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: self_bidirected_excluded_asymmetric_with_directed_self_cycles
explanation: Item iii ($E^{\sm W}$) explicitly allows self-loops, with the footnote "Note that this may introduce self-cycles." Item iv ($L^{\sm W}$), by contrast, requires "$\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$", silently excluding self-bidirected edges that bifurcations through $W$ would otherwise create.

Concrete corner case: take $v \in V \sm W$ and $w_1, w_2 \in W$ such that $G$ contains the structure
    $v \hut w_1 \hus w_2 \tuh v$ (i.e., $w_1 \tuh v$, $w_1 \hus w_2$, and $w_2 \tuh v$ all in $G$).
This is a bona-fide bifurcation in the sense of the displayed formula, with $\ul{v} = \ol{v} = v$, $k = 2$, $n = 2$, and intermediates $w_1, w_2 \in W$. The natural reading would add a self-bidirected $v \huh v$ to $L^{\sm W}$, capturing "confounding of $v$ with itself through latent path in $W$". The "$\ul{v} \neq \ol{v}$" condition discards this case.

The asymmetry with item iii's footnote is jarring: the directed case acknowledges self-cycle creation as a feature, while the bidirected case rules it out without justification in the definition itself. If the surrounding CDMG framework forbids self-bidirected edges by convention, that justifies the asymmetry — but the definition doesn't say so. Downstream proofs about which confounding paths are "preserved" by marginalization should account for the fact that confounding paths returning to their starting node are silently dropped, while directed cycles returning to the starting node are kept.
END_SUBTLETY
```

rows.addition_to_the_LN:
[bifurcation_optional_v3_phrasing_ambiguity] Part 2 of the remark asserts two separate biconditionals, corresponding to reading the statement once with the parenthetical clauses omitted and once with them included. Explicitly:
(a) For $v_1, v_2 \in G \sm W$: there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ if and only if there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G^{\sm W}$.
(b) For $v_1, v_2, v_3 \in G \sm W$: there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ with source $v_3$ in $G$ if and only if there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ with source $v_3$ in $G^{\sm W}$.

wording_check.output:
SUBTLETY:
id: optionally_v3_phrasing_creates_dual_claim
explanation: Item 2 reads:

> "For $v_1,v_2 \in G \sm W$ (and, optionally, $v_3 \in G\sm W$): there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ (with source $v_3$) in $G$ if and only if there is a bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ (with source $v_3$) in $G^{\sm W}$."

The parenthetical "(and, optionally, $v_3 \in G\sm W$)" combined with the embedded "(with source $v_3$)" makes the statement ambiguous: it bundles two distinct claims into one sentence.

- Reading A — two separate claims: (i) for all $v_1,v_2 \in G \sm W$, an unsourced bifurcation between $v_1,v_2$ exists in $G$ iff in $G^{\sm W}$; (ii) for all $v_1,v_2,v_3 \in G\sm W$, a bifurcation between $v_1,v_2$ with source $v_3$ exists in $G$ iff in $G^{\sm W}$.
- Reading B — a single claim where $v_3$ is universally quantified and the "(with source $v_3$)" is mandatory wherever it appears.

"Optionally" is mathematically unusual; a formalization (or a careful reader) has to commit to one interpretation, and the two readings are not the same claim. In particular, reading A includes the unsourced version with its existential over sources, which is the strictly stronger statement.
END_SUBTLETY

SUBTLETY:
id: bifurcation_no_source_existential_admits_w_source
explanation: Under reading A of item 2 (the "(with source $v_3$)" optional), the unsourced version reads "bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ iff bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G^{\sm W}$" with $v_1,v_2 \in G \sm W$. The LHS implicitly quantifies the source existentially over all of $V(G)$, including $W$. The RHS quantifies over $V(G^{\sm W}) = V(G)\sm W$, which by construction contains no $W$ vertices.

Concrete corner case: suppose the only bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$ in $G$ is the fork $v_1 \leftarrow w \rightarrow v_2$ with $w \in W$ (no other common-source vertex in $V(G)$, no bidirected edge in $G$). The LHS is true. For the RHS to also be true, $G^{\sm W}$ must contain something that counts as a "bifurcation between $v_1$ and $v_2$" — typically the bidirected edge $v_1 \leftrightarrow v_2$ that marginalization is meant to introduce.

So the unsourced biconditional silently relies on: (a) "bifurcation" being defined inclusively enough to count bidirected edges as bifurcations (with an implicit unobserved source), and (b) the marginalization $G \mapsto G^{\sm W}$ actually inserting such bidirected edges for marginalized common causes. If the document's local notion of "bifurcation" is the strict three-vertex fork $v_1 \leftarrow v_3 \rightarrow v_2$ with $v_3$ a vertex of the graph, then under that strict reading the unsourced direction fails on this corner case, and a downstream proof that walks through the unsourced biconditional would silently rely on the more permissive definition.
END_SUBTLETY
